questions.question: Arrange the motor hierarchy from highest to lowest level:
questions.explanation: The hierarchy table from the lecture: Upper motor neurons (command specification) → Basal ganglia (movement initiation gating) → Cerebellum (error correction) → Lower levels (spinal cord reflexes, final output).

questions.question: Order the stages of the dot motion paradigm from stimulus to response:
items: Neural firing rate scales with coherence
correct_order: Neural firing rate scales with coherence
questions.explanation: In the dot motion paradigm: a visual display with varying coherence is presented → neurons fire proportionally to coherence level (graded conversion) → firing accumulates to a decision threshold → the motor response is executed.

questions.question: Order the progression of Bernstein's signature experiment from specific to general:
items: Same distinctive features appear with elbow/mouth writing
Motor program is not tied to specific muscles
correct_order: Same distinctive features appear with elbow/mouth writing
Motor program is not tied to specific muscles
questions.explanation: The logic: you write with your hand → the same features appear with other body parts → this proves the program isn't muscle-specific → therefore motor programs exist at an abstract, effector-independent level.

questions.question: Order the dorsal/ventral stream processing from input to function:
items: Streams diverge to temporal or parietal cortex
correct_order: Streams diverge to temporal or parietal cortex
questions.explanation: Visual processing flows: input enters occipital cortex → basic features processed → streams diverge (ventral to temporal, dorsal to parietal) → ventral stream identifies objects ('what'), dorsal stream guides actions ('where/how').

questions.question: Order the putamen learning experiment from novel to learned:
questions.explanation: When a novel sensorimotor mapping is introduced → the putamen activates strongly during the learning phase → the mapping becomes automatic with practice → putamen activation decreases as the skill is consolidated.

questions.question: Order the types of apraxia from most basic to most complex:
items: Ideomotor apraxia (can't voluntarily perform single actions)
correct_order: Ideomotor apraxia (can't voluntarily perform single actions)
questions.explanation: From most basic to most complex: verbal apraxia affects single speech movements, ideomotor apraxia affects single voluntary skilled actions, and ideational apraxia affects sequencing complex multi-step action plans.

questions.question: Order the SRT (serial reaction time) task steps:
items: Reaction times increase when sequence removed
Stimuli appear in hidden repeating sequence
correct_order: Stimuli appear in hidden repeating sequence
Reaction times increase when sequence removed
questions.explanation: In the SRT task: stimuli follow a hidden repeating pattern → participants respond to each → reaction times decrease (showing implicit sequence learning) → when the sequence is removed, reaction times increase, confirming learning occurred.

questions.question: Order the cerebellar error correction process during a reaching movement:
items: Corrective signal sent to adjust movement trajectory
Copy of motor command sent to cerebellum
Proprioceptive feedback about actual limb position arrives
correct_order: Copy of motor command sent to cerebellum
Proprioceptive feedback about actual limb position arrives
Corrective signal sent to adjust movement trajectory
questions.explanation: During reaching: a copy of the motor command goes to the cerebellum → sensory feedback about actual position arrives → the cerebellum compares and detects any mismatch → it sends corrective signals to adjust the trajectory in real-time.

questions.question: Order the Tanji SMA experiment steps:
items: Confirms SMA is necessary for learned sequences
correct_order: Confirms SMA is necessary for learned sequences
questions.explanation: In Tanji's experiment: monkeys learned specific sequences → SMA neurons fired in sequence-specific patterns → when the SMA was pharmacologically inactivated, sequence performance was disrupted → confirming the SMA is necessary for learned movement sequences.

questions.question: Order the mirror neuron discovery from observation to theory:
items: Theory: mirror neurons underlie action understanding
Neurons in F5 fire during monkey's own grasping
Human mirror system found to be more widely distributed
Same neurons fire when observing another's grasping
correct_order: Neurons in F5 fire during monkey's own grasping
Same neurons fire when observing another's grasping
Human mirror system found to be more widely distributed
Theory: mirror neurons underlie action understanding
questions.explanation: The mirror neuron story: first found that F5 neurons fire during the monkey's own actions → then found the same neurons fire during observation → studies showed humans have a more widely distributed mirror system → theories developed about action understanding.
